reviews.summary: In this paper, authors argue that for multimodal LLMs, the previous input formulation is too rigid: either needs to specify the region models should focus on, or brings a verbose object description to refer to the region. A more natural referring expression strategy can help models better understand where the model should pay more attention to and the intent of the input questions. The proposed approach, called Localized Symbolic Knowledge Distillation (LSKD), aims to provide more natural referring expressions. It involves providing literal descriptions of images to a large language model and allowing the model to connect these descriptions with a holistic understanding of the scene. In addition, localized references to specific regions within the image are provided to encourage the model to generate commonsense inferences about those regions. This method effectively distills the large language model's capacity for global and local scene understanding. They demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach by achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on localized visual reasoning benchmarks and conducting human evaluations.
reviews.strengths:
Novel research question to distill visual commonsense: Distilling visual commonsense is an interesting question that previous works under-explore. The distilled visual commonsense could provide very useful information for understanding complex situations covered in datasets like VCR, which will make the reasoning more grounded and reliable. Also, this is the first work about automatically acquiring visual commonsense to my best knowledge. 

Comprehensive experimental results on downstream tasks: I'm very glad to see the distilled knowledge could further help base models on downstream tasks like VCR, Sherlock and VisualCOMET. Also, the small models can even outperform ChatGPT with the help of LSKD when describing the useful information in localized reasoning process. Besides, authors design experiments to examine the effect of steps in LSKD generation methods, which further make the argument stronger.
reviews.weaknesses:
Lack of comparison with Sherlock and VisualCOMET: Although the current experiments support that LSKD is helpful for downstream tasks, I'm still a bit confused if LSKD is better than Sherlock and VisualCOMET knowledge bases. These two datasets are both large-scale and of great quality. It's better to compare with competitive resources, instead of just showing that considering external knowledge will help. 

Comparing VL models and ChatGPT is unfair: Although ChatGPT is prompted with verbalizers, it still can only accept text information, which may ignore many visual information in the images. Especially for datasets like VCR, answering questions may need a very careful observation to some detailed places, which is what VL models like BLIP-2 is better at.

Presentation in introduction section is confusing: I don't quite understand the relation between the first and second half of the introduction section. For the first half, I think the authors are trying to say a better referring expression is important. But in the second half (which is the core part of the paper), they just focus on how to distill visual commonsense knowledge. They are a bit connected because both parts mention localized reference, but it has very loose connection with discussion about the quality of referring expression.
reviews.questions:
1. Is there any way to apply this method to the datasets with no bounding box annotations? That will further improve the scalability of this method.
2. Is there any study to explore the correlation between the ration of error training instances and the downstream task performance?
3. Could you also apply LSKD to models like CLIP? It seems that in Table 2, they just use BLIP-2 ViT-G to show the effectiveness of LSKD.
4. More qualitative error analysis is welcome in the modified version.
reviews.limitations: The limitations about introducing errors in the intermediate steps are reasonable.
reviews.rating: 8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, authors argue that for multimodal LLMs, the previous input formulation is too rigid: either needs to specify the region models should focus on, or brings a verbose object description to refer to the region. A more natural referring expression strategy can help models better understand where the model should pay more attention to and the intent of the input questions. The proposed approach, called Localized Symbolic Knowledge Distillation (LSKD), aims to provide more natural referring expressions. It involves providing literal descriptions of images to a large language model and allowing the model to connect these descriptions with a holistic understanding of the scene. In addition, localized references to specific regions within the image are provided to encourage the model to generate commonsense inferences about those regions. This method effectively distills the large language model's capacity for global and local scene understanding. They demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach by achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on localized visual reasoning benchmarks and conducting human evaluations.

Strengths:
Novel research question to distill visual commonsense: Distilling visual commonsense is an interesting question that previous works under-explore. The distilled visual commonsense could provide very useful information for understanding complex situations covered in datasets like VCR, which will make the reasoning more grounded and reliable. Also, this is the first work about automatically acquiring visual commonsense to my best knowledge. 

Comprehensive experimental results on downstream tasks: I'm very glad to see the distilled knowledge could further help base models on downstream tasks like VCR, Sherlock and VisualCOMET. Also, the small models can even outperform ChatGPT with the help of LSKD when describing the useful information in localized reasoning process. Besides, authors design experiments to examine the effect of steps in LSKD generation methods, which further make the argument stronger.

Weaknesses:
Lack of comparison with Sherlock and VisualCOMET: Although the current experiments support that LSKD is helpful for downstream tasks, I'm still a bit confused if LSKD is better than Sherlock and VisualCOMET knowledge bases. These two datasets are both large-scale and of great quality. It's better to compare with competitive resources, instead of just showing that considering external knowledge will help. 

Comparing VL models and ChatGPT is unfair: Although ChatGPT is prompted with verbalizers, it still can only accept text information, which may ignore many visual information in the images. Especially for datasets like VCR, answering questions may need a very careful observation to some detailed places, which is what VL models like BLIP-2 is better at.

Presentation in introduction section is confusing: I don't quite understand the relation between the first and second half of the introduction section. For the first half, I think the authors are trying to say a better referring expression is important. But in the second half (which is the core part of the paper), they just focus on how to distill visual commonsense knowledge. They are a bit connected because both parts mention localized reference, but it has very loose connection with discussion about the quality of referring expression.

Questions:
1. Is there any way to apply this method to the datasets with no bounding box annotations? That will further improve the scalability of this method.
2. Is there any study to explore the correlation between the ration of error training instances and the downstream task performance?
3. Could you also apply LSKD to models like CLIP? It seems that in Table 2, they just use BLIP-2 ViT-G to show the effectiveness of LSKD.
4. More qualitative error analysis is welcome in the modified version.

Limitations:
The limitations about introducing errors in the intermediate steps are reasonable.

Rating:
8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review Report

**Summary:**
This paper addresses a critical limitation in current multimodal Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically the overly rigid nature of their input formulations. Existing methods often demand precise region specifications or cumbersome object descriptions to refer to visual areas. The authors propose that a more intuitive strategy for referring expressions could significantly enhance models' comprehension of salient regions and the underlying intent of input queries. To this end, they introduce Localized Symbolic Knowledge Distillation (LSKD), an approach designed to generate more natural referring expressions. LSKD operates by feeding literal image descriptions into a powerful LLM, enabling it to construct a holistic understanding of the scene. Concurrently, localized references to specific image regions are provided, prompting the model to generate commonsense inferences pertinent to those areas. This methodology effectively distills the LLM's capacity for both global and local scene interpretation. The paper substantiates LSKD's efficacy by demonstrating state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on localized visual reasoning benchmarks and through comprehensive human evaluations.

**Strengths:**

*   **Pioneering Research into Visual Commonsense Distillation:** The investigation into distilling visual commonsense represents a genuinely novel and underexplored research avenue. This extracted knowledge holds substantial promise for enriching the understanding of complex scenarios, such as those found in datasets like VCR, thereby fostering more grounded and reliable reasoning processes. To my knowledge, this work stands out as the first to automate the acquisition of such visual commonsense.
*   **Thorough Experimental Validation on Downstream Tasks:** I am particularly impressed by the extensive experimental results, which compellingly demonstrate LSKD's ability to boost the performance of base models across various downstream tasks, including VCR, Sherlock, and VisualCOMET. Notably, even smaller models, when augmented with LSKD, prove capable of surpassing ChatGPT in articulating useful information during localized reasoning. Furthermore, the authors' inclusion of ablation studies examining the different steps within the LSKD generation pipeline significantly strengthens the paper's arguments and findings.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Insufficient Comparison with Established Knowledge Bases:** While the experiments clearly show that incorporating LSKD is beneficial for downstream tasks, there remains an ambiguity regarding LSKD's comparative performance against well-established knowledge bases like Sherlock and VisualCOMET. These datasets are known for their large scale and high quality. A more direct comparison with such competitive resources would be valuable, rather than solely illustrating the general utility of external knowledge.
*   **Problematic Comparison Between VL Models and ChatGPT:** The comparison between vision-language (VL) models and ChatGPT raises concerns about fairness. Although ChatGPT is utilized with verbalizers, its fundamental limitation to text-only input means it inherently overlooks a wealth of visual information present in images. This is particularly critical for datasets like VCR, where answering questions often necessitates meticulous observation of fine-grained visual details—an area where VL models such as BLIP-2 inherently possess an advantage.
*   **Confusing Narrative Flow in the Introduction:** The introduction section suffers from a lack of clear thematic connection between its two halves. The initial part seems to emphasize the importance of improved referring expressions, while the latter, which delves into the core of the paper, primarily focuses on the distillation of visual commonsense knowledge. Although localized references provide a minor link, the overall discussion feels disconnected from the initial premise about the quality of referring expressions.

**Questions:**

1.  Is there a potential methodology to adapt LSKD for use with datasets that lack explicit bounding box annotations? Such an extension would significantly enhance the scalability and applicability of the method.
2.  Have the authors conducted any investigations into the correlation between the proportion of erroneous instances introduced during training and the subsequent performance on downstream tasks?
3.  Could LSKD be applied to other vision-language models, such as CLIP? Table 2 primarily showcases the effectiveness of LSKD in conjunction with BLIP-2 ViT-G, and exploring its broader applicability would be insightful.
4.  A more comprehensive qualitative error analysis would be a valuable addition to a revised version of the paper.

**Limitations:**

The authors provide a reasonable and well-justified discussion regarding the potential for errors to be introduced during the intermediate steps of their method.

---

**Rating: 8: Strong Accept**

This paper presents a technically robust contribution with genuinely novel ideas. Its potential impact is excellent, either within a specific domain or across several, backed by thorough evaluation, provision of resources, and good reproducibility. No significant ethical concerns are identified.

reviews.summary: This paper aims to build a instruction following model for **localized** visual commonsense reasoning. The work differs from existing works in that it is able to reason about localized image regions with boxes, without using complicated referring expressions. The paper first collects data by distilling LLMs, i.e., prompting GPT using verbalized image descriptions, which is a concatenation of 3 descriptors: global, local, and QA pairs. Then, the data collected in the first stage is filtered by a critic filter trained using human annotations. Finally, the filtered data is used to train a BLIP-2 model. Experiments are done on 3 datasets for the localized visual reasoning task, showing the performance of the proposed method.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is clearly motivated and the problem studied is well described.
- The dataset collection method is well designed. Using (global, local, QA) descriptors to prompt LLM in order to generate question, answer and rationales.
- The effectiveness of the critic filtering is well-analyzed and clearly shown in Fig 3.
- The paper is well written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
My major concern is about experiments. The current results may not be very extensive to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.
- Box size is an important factor, since the task is localized reasoning. How does the model perform on large and small objects? How does this compare to other methods? First, it would be helpful to describe the distribution of the bbox sizes in the generated data. Second, in the final evaluation, it is also helpful to study the model performance change wrt different box sizes.
- three descriptors (global, local, QA) are used to prompt LLMs. Is there a study to show the contribution of each of the three? Since QA is not as widely used as the other two, additional discussions/results showing its effectiveness would be good.
- Very limited baselines are compared with. In table 2, only CLIP is compared. In table 3, only ChatGPT. More baselines should be considered. For example, for tab 2, while zero-shot baselines are hard to find, it is still beneficial to show some non zero-shot methods (methods on the leaderboard of these datasets). Although the model performance is not directly comparable, it can give readers some sense of how well the model performs.
- (potential extension) While the contribution on the data collection/distillation part is strong, the contribution on the model part is weak. Basically, BLIP-2 is directly used and trained on the collected data. Could there be some model design specific for handling “localized” information? Moreover, the “localized” reasoning in the current paper is constrained to color-coded bbox, while more general forms like free-form masks can be a further extension.
- The work is in principle similar to LLaVA, extending it to be “localized”. Could the authors discuss the difference with LLaVA in more detail?
- The title suggests “symbolic”, how is this work related to “symbolic”? I feel this is mis-leading.
- some typos: L168 QA-MSE, L146 QAR is not defined.
reviews.questions: See weakness.
reviews.limitations: N/A.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper aims to build a instruction following model for **localized** visual commonsense reasoning. The work differs from existing works in that it is able to reason about localized image regions with boxes, without using complicated referring expressions. The paper first collects data by distilling LLMs, i.e., prompting GPT using verbalized image descriptions, which is a concatenation of 3 descriptors: global, local, and QA pairs. Then, the data collected in the first stage is filtered by a critic filter trained using human annotations. Finally, the filtered data is used to train a BLIP-2 model. Experiments are done on 3 datasets for the localized visual reasoning task, showing the performance of the proposed method.

Strengths:
- The paper is clearly motivated and the problem studied is well described.
- The dataset collection method is well designed. Using (global, local, QA) descriptors to prompt LLM in order to generate question, answer and rationales.
- The effectiveness of the critic filtering is well-analyzed and clearly shown in Fig 3.
- The paper is well written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
My major concern is about experiments. The current results may not be very extensive to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.
- Box size is an important factor, since the task is localized reasoning. How does the model perform on large and small objects? How does this compare to other methods? First, it would be helpful to describe the distribution of the bbox sizes in the generated data. Second, in the final evaluation, it is also helpful to study the model performance change wrt different box sizes.
- three descriptors (global, local, QA) are used to prompt LLMs. Is there a study to show the contribution of each of the three? Since QA is not as widely used as the other two, additional discussions/results showing its effectiveness would be good.
- Very limited baselines are compared with. In table 2, only CLIP is compared. In table 3, only ChatGPT. More baselines should be considered. For example, for tab 2, while zero-shot baselines are hard to find, it is still beneficial to show some non zero-shot methods (methods on the leaderboard of these datasets). Although the model performance is not directly comparable, it can give readers some sense of how well the model performs.
- (potential extension) While the contribution on the data collection/distillation part is strong, the contribution on the model part is weak. Basically, BLIP-2 is directly used and trained on the collected data. Could there be some model design specific for handling “localized” information? Moreover, the “localized” reasoning in the current paper is constrained to color-coded bbox, while more general forms like free-form masks can be a further extension.
- The work is in principle similar to LLaVA, extending it to be “localized”. Could the authors discuss the difference with LLaVA in more detail?
- The title suggests “symbolic”, how is this work related to “symbolic”? I feel this is mis-leading.
- some typos: L168 QA-MSE, L146 QAR is not defined.

Questions:
See weakness.

Limitations:
N/A.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
**Summary:**
This paper introduces an instruction-following model tailored for **localized** visual commonsense reasoning. A key differentiating aspect of this work is its capability to reason about specific image regions, delineated by bounding boxes, without requiring complex referring expressions. The proposed methodology initiates with a data collection phase involving the distillation of large language models (LLMs). This is achieved by prompting GPT with verbalized image descriptions, which are a concatenation of three distinct components: global context, local region details, and question-answer pairs. Subsequently, the data generated in this initial stage undergoes a rigorous filtering process utilizing a critic model trained on human-annotated examples. Finally, this refined dataset is employed to train a BLIP-2 model. The efficacy of the proposed method is evaluated through experiments conducted on three datasets specifically designed for localized visual reasoning tasks.

**Strengths:**
*   The paper clearly establishes its motivation, and the problem under investigation is articulated with precision.
*   The methodology for collecting the dataset is thoughtfully designed, particularly the strategy of using combined global, local, and QA descriptors to prompt the LLM for generating questions, answers, and accompanying rationales.
*   The effectiveness of the critic filtering mechanism is thoroughly analyzed and its impact is visibly demonstrated in Figure 3.
*   The manuscript is well-structured and written, making it easy to follow and comprehend.

**Weaknesses:**
My primary concern revolves around the experimental validation. The current set of results may not be sufficiently extensive to comprehensively showcase the full effectiveness of the proposed method.
*   **Bounding Box Size Analysis:** Given that the task focuses on localized reasoning, the dimensions of the bounding boxes are a critical factor. It would be highly beneficial to:
    *   Provide a description of the distribution of bounding box sizes within the generated dataset.
    *   Investigate how the model's performance changes in the final evaluation when applied to objects of varying sizes (e.g., large versus small objects).
    *   Compare this size-dependent performance with that of other existing methods.
*   **Contribution of Descriptors:** The LLM prompting strategy employs three distinct descriptors: global, local, and QA. An analysis demonstrating the individual contribution of each of these three descriptors would be valuable. Specifically, since QA pairs are not as universally adopted as the other two, additional discussions or results highlighting their specific effectiveness would strengthen the paper.
*   **Limited Baselines:** The experimental comparisons are quite restricted.
    *   In Table 2, only CLIP is used as a baseline.
    *   In Table 3, only ChatGPT is compared.
    More diverse baselines should be incorporated. For instance, while finding direct zero-shot baselines might be challenging for Table 2, it would still be advantageous to include some non-zero-shot methods (e.g., models that perform well on the leaderboards of these datasets). Although direct performance comparisons might not be feasible, this would offer readers a better contextual understanding of the proposed model's performance level.
*   **Model Contribution and Potential Extensions:** While the contribution stemming from the data collection and distillation process is robust, the contribution on the model architecture itself appears less significant, as a standard BLIP-2 model is directly utilized and trained on the collected data.
    *   Could there be specific model designs or modifications tailored to more effectively handle "localized" information?
    *   Furthermore, the current interpretation of "localized" reasoning is confined to color-coded bounding boxes. Extending this to more generalized forms of localization, such as free-form masks, could represent a promising future direction.
*   **Comparison with LLaVA:** The work shares conceptual similarities with LLaVA, particularly in its extension to localized contexts. A more detailed discussion from the authors elucidating the precise differences and unique contributions relative to LLaVA would be beneficial.
*   **Title Terminology:** The inclusion of "symbolic" in the title appears potentially misleading given the current scope of the work. Clarification on its relevance or a reconsideration of the title might be warranted.
*   **Typographical Errors:**
    *   Line 168: "QA-MSE" needs review.
    *   Line 146: The acronym "QAR" is used without a prior definition.

**Questions:**
See Weaknesses.

**Limitations:**
N/A.

**Rating:**
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a framework that can generate global, local, and dynamic descriptions. ChatGPT can generate question-and-answer pairs containing specific regions or descriptive phrases with the proposed tricks. A critic model is trained to filter the generated data. Finally, the localized corpus is used to fine-tune the vision language model.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper proposes a way to acquire localized commonsense knowledge data and enable the vision and language model to answer localized visual commonsense questions. The workload of this work is impressive.

2. The fine-tuned vision language model achieves promising zero-shot performance for three localized visual reasoning tasks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The proposed framework is more like engineering work. The proposed method to generate localized data pairs is straightforward, and the way to enable the local-level question-answering ability is not novel, limiting its scientific contribution.

2. There are too many tricks, including filtering by critic model and prompts for acquiring question-answering pairs, which decreases the reproducibility and robustness of the whole pipeline. Furthermore, the selection of the vision-language model and large language model, and hyperparameters for each model in each step increase the complexity of the proposed pipeline, and the final vision-language model may be sensitive to the generated data, which makes it hard to iterative improve the final model's performance by improving any step in this pipeline.
reviews.questions: This paper proposes an impressive engineering framework. It would be better to analyze the impact of the quality and amount of the generated data on the final performance of the visual language model.
reviews.limitations: I did not find a potential negative societal impact of this work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a framework that can generate global, local, and dynamic descriptions. ChatGPT can generate question-and-answer pairs containing specific regions or descriptive phrases with the proposed tricks. A critic model is trained to filter the generated data. Finally, the localized corpus is used to fine-tune the vision language model.

Strengths:
1. This paper proposes a way to acquire localized commonsense knowledge data and enable the vision and language model to answer localized visual commonsense questions. The workload of this work is impressive.

2. The fine-tuned vision language model achieves promising zero-shot performance for three localized visual reasoning tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. The proposed framework is more like engineering work. The proposed method to generate localized data pairs is straightforward, and the way to enable the local-level question-answering ability is not novel, limiting its scientific contribution.

2. There are too many tricks, including filtering by critic model and prompts for acquiring question-answering pairs, which decreases the reproducibility and robustness of the whole pipeline. Furthermore, the selection of the vision-language model and large language model, and hyperparameters for each model in each step increase the complexity of the proposed pipeline, and the final vision-language model may be sensitive to the generated data, which makes it hard to iterative improve the final model's performance by improving any step in this pipeline.

Questions:
This paper proposes an impressive engineering framework. It would be better to analyze the impact of the quality and amount of the generated data on the final performance of the visual language model.

Limitations:
I did not find a potential negative societal impact of this work.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Here is the rewritten peer review:

## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a comprehensive framework designed to create descriptions that capture visual information at global, local, and dynamic levels. It employs a large language model, specifically ChatGPT, to generate question-and-answer pairs that are focused on particular visual regions or descriptive phrases, utilizing a set of tailored prompting strategies. A subsequent critic model is then trained to effectively filter and refine this synthetically generated data. Ultimately, this curated, localized corpus is leveraged to fine-tune a vision-language model.

**Strengths:**
1.  The authors have devised an effective methodology for acquiring localized commonsense knowledge data, which in turn successfully enables vision-language models to address visual commonsense questions with a localized focus. The sheer scale and diligent effort invested in this work are genuinely impressive.
2.  The fine-tuned vision-language model demonstrates a promising level of zero-shot performance across three distinct tasks that require localized visual reasoning.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  The proposed framework primarily manifests as an engineering solution rather than offering a substantial theoretical or scientific breakthrough. The strategy employed for generating localized data pairs is relatively straightforward, and the concept of empowering models with local-level question-answering capabilities is not fundamentally novel, thereby somewhat restricting its profound scientific contribution.
2.  The pipeline incorporates a considerable number of heuristic "tricks," including the use of a critic model for data filtering and specific prompt engineering techniques for generating question-answering pairs. This reliance on numerous specialized configurations raises legitimate concerns regarding the overall reproducibility and robustness of the entire system. Furthermore, the specific choices made for the vision-language model, the large language model, and the various hyperparameters at each stage significantly increase the complexity of the proposed architecture. This inherent complexity suggests that the final performance of the vision-language model could be highly sensitive to the characteristics of the generated data, making it challenging to incrementally improve the model's performance by optimizing individual components within the pipeline.

**Questions:**
Given the notable practical implementation presented, it would greatly enhance the paper to include a detailed analysis of how the quality and quantity of the generated data influence the ultimate performance of the vision-language model.

**Limitations:**
I did not identify any potential negative societal implications arising from this research.

**Rating:**
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation.

Summary:
This paper proposes a framework that can generate global, local, and dynamic descriptions. ChatGPT can generate question-and-answer pairs containing specific regions or descriptive phrases with the proposed tricks. A critic model is trained to filter the generated data. Finally, the localized corpus is used to fine-tune the vision language model.

Strengths:
1. This paper proposes a way to acquire localized commonsense knowledge data and enable the vision and language model to answer localized visual commonsense questions. The workload of this work is impressive.

2. The fine-tuned vision language model achieves promising zero-shot performance for three localized visual reasoning tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. The proposed framework is more like engineering work. The proposed method to generate localized data pairs is straightforward, and the way to enable the local-level question-answering ability is not novel, limiting its scientific contribution.

2. There are too many tricks, including filtering by critic model and prompts for acquiring question-answering pairs, which decreases the reproducibility and robustness of the whole pipeline. Furthermore, the selection of the vision-language model and large language model, and hyperparameters for each model in each step increase the complexity of the proposed pipeline, and the final vision-language model may be sensitive to the generated data, which makes it hard to iterative improve the final model's performance by improving any step in this pipeline.

Questions:
This paper proposes an impressive engineering framework. It would be better to analyze the impact of the quality and amount of the generated data on the final performance of the visual language model.

Limitations:
I did not find a potential negative societal impact of this work.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Localized Visual Commonsense models that enhance vision-language (VL) models by allowing users to specify specific regions within images. The authors train their model by collecting commonsense knowledge from a large language model (LLM) using global literal image descriptions and automatically generating local literal region descriptions from VL models. They also use a critic model to select high-quality examples. The results show that training on this localized commonsense corpus improves the performance of VL models of passing generated referring expressions to an LLM.
reviews.strengths:
+ The paper is well-motivated. The introduction of Localized Visual Commonsense models represents a novel solution to build a more general multi-modal model.

+ The constructed data is of large scale, which contains 1M samples over 250K images. 

+ The extensive experiments show that the proposed dataset can support model to perform better zero-shot inference.
reviews.weaknesses:
- How does this Dataset reflect knowledge? Since ChatGPT's input is based on factual descriptions of images, how can we ensure the questions generated by ChatGPT are related to external knowledge? On the other hand, how do the authors define what are knowledge-related questions?

- For some knowledge-related questions, it seems that ChatGPT needs to guess the state of the subject in the image. Could this process result in hallucination issues? I notice that there's a critic filtering in the method, which rates the results by training on annotated data. What types of patterns is this model expected to learn, and under what circumstances does it consider the generated data to be inadequate? From Section 2.3, it seems to be judging visual correctness, but how can the correctness of the generated knowledge be determined?

For example, in Fig1, the model assumes that [0] and [2] are teaching [1]. This is a guess made by ChatGPT, as no similar descriptions are generated by the visual models, indicating that they cannot capture this information. So how would the critic model determine if this kind of guess is based on reality or is just a hallucination?
reviews.questions: My major concern with this work is how to guarantee the dataset quality since there are a lot of automatic generation methods in dataset construction. So I would like to hear more illustrations about that.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper introduces Localized Visual Commonsense models that enhance vision-language (VL) models by allowing users to specify specific regions within images. The authors train their model by collecting commonsense knowledge from a large language model (LLM) using global literal image descriptions and automatically generating local literal region descriptions from VL models. They also use a critic model to select high-quality examples. The results show that training on this localized commonsense corpus improves the performance of VL models of passing generated referring expressions to an LLM.

Strengths:
+ The paper is well-motivated. The introduction of Localized Visual Commonsense models represents a novel solution to build a more general multi-modal model.

+ The constructed data is of large scale, which contains 1M samples over 250K images. 

+ The extensive experiments show that the proposed dataset can support model to perform better zero-shot inference.

Weaknesses:
- How does this Dataset reflect knowledge? Since ChatGPT's input is based on factual descriptions of images, how can we ensure the questions generated by ChatGPT are related to external knowledge? On the other hand, how do the authors define what are knowledge-related questions?

- For some knowledge-related questions, it seems that ChatGPT needs to guess the state of the subject in the image. Could this process result in hallucination issues? I notice that there's a critic filtering in the method, which rates the results by training on annotated data. What types of patterns is this model expected to learn, and under what circumstances does it consider the generated data to be inadequate? From Section 2.3, it seems to be judging visual correctness, but how can the correctness of the generated knowledge be determined?

For example, in Fig1, the model assumes that [0] and [2] are teaching [1]. This is a guess made by ChatGPT, as no similar descriptions are generated by the visual models, indicating that they cannot capture this information. So how would the critic model determine if this kind of guess is based on reality or is just a hallucination?

Questions:
My major concern with this work is how to guarantee the dataset quality since there are a lot of automatic generation methods in dataset construction. So I would like to hear more illustrations about that.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
**Summary:**
This paper introduces an innovative concept: Localized Visual Commonsense models, designed to augment existing vision-language (VL) frameworks by enabling them to process information pertaining to specific, user-defined regions within images. The authors detail a methodology for constructing a training corpus, which involves extracting commonsense knowledge from a large language model (LLM) using global literal image descriptions, and then automatically generating localized literal descriptions for specific regions via VL models. To ensure data quality, a critic model is employed to filter for high-quality examples. The experimental findings indicate that training with this novel localized commonsense corpus effectively enhances the performance of VL models, particularly when generating referring expressions that are subsequently processed by an LLM.

**Strengths:**
*   The fundamental premise of the paper is exceptionally strong and timely. The introduction of Localized Visual Commonsense models represents a genuinely novel and well-motivated approach towards developing more generalized and nuanced multi-modal AI systems.
*   A notable advantage of this work is the impressive scale of the constructed dataset, which encompasses a substantial 1 million samples derived from 250,000 unique images.
*   The authors have conducted a thorough series of experiments, which convincingly demonstrate that the proposed dataset effectively supports and improves models' capabilities for zero-shot inference.

**Weaknesses:**
*   My primary concern revolves around the intrinsic quality and reliability of the automatically generated dataset, particularly regarding its capacity to truly reflect "knowledge." Given that the input to ChatGPT for knowledge extraction is stated to be factual descriptions of images, it remains unclear how the generated questions are guaranteed to tap into external commonsense knowledge rather than merely rephrasing visual observations. A clearer definition or operationalization of what constitutes a "knowledge-related question" within this context would be highly beneficial.
*   A significant issue arises concerning the potential for hallucination within the generated knowledge. It appears that for certain knowledge-related inquiries, ChatGPT might be making speculative inferences about the state or actions of subjects in an image. This raises questions about the factual accuracy of such generated content. While the paper mentions a critic filtering mechanism trained on annotated data, its precise function needs further elaboration. What specific patterns or characteristics does this critic model learn to identify? Under what circumstances does it deem generated data to be unsuitable or incorrect? Section 2.3 suggests it primarily assesses visual correctness, but how does it extend to validating the correctness of *inferred knowledge* or abstract statements? The example provided in Figure 1, where the model presumes elements [0] and [2] are teaching [1] despite no visual model descriptions capturing this, perfectly illustrates this challenge. How would the critic model objectively distinguish between a valid, knowledge-based deduction and a pure hallucination in such instances?

**Rating: 5: Borderline accept**

This paper presents a technically robust approach with a compelling motivation and strong empirical evidence supporting its utility. While the concerns regarding the nuanced distinction between factual observation, knowledge inference, and potential hallucination within the automatically generated dataset are significant and necessitate further clarification and perhaps additional validation, the overall strengths of the work—especially its novelty and the impressive scale of the data construction—outweigh these reservations. I recommend acceptance, with the expectation that the authors will thoroughly address these points in their final revision.

Weaknesses:

- For some knowledge-related questions, it seems that ChatGPT needs to guess the state of the subject in the image. Could this process result in hallucination issues? I notice that there's a critic filtering in the method, which rates the results by training on annotated data. What types of patterns is this model expected to learn, and under what circumstances does it consider the generated data to be inadequate? From Section 2.3, it seems to be judging visual correctness, but how can the correctness of the generated knowledge be determined?

For example, in Fig1, the model assumes that [0] and [2] are teaching [1]. This is a guess made by ChatGPT, as no similar descriptions are generated by the visual models, indicating that they cannot capture this information. So how would the critic model determine if this kind of guess is based on reality or is just a hallucination?

title: Django: Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration

reviews.summary: The key idea of this paper is to propose Django, an adaptation of the trigger reverse-engineering technique used for detecting backdoored models in classification to object detection models. The paper shows that existing trigger reverse-engineering techniques are ineffective in object detection and finds that loss misalignment is the primary reason for the less effectiveness. The paper then proposes a new loss function that leverages Focal loss, a well-studied loss function in object detection, against compromised object detection models. In evaluation, Django is more effective than the baseline methods in backdoor detection with less computational overhead. The paper shows Django is effective against a few adaptive attacks.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper proposes a backdoor detection method for object detection models.
2. The paper presents why existing detection (trigger reverse-engineering) methods are ineffective.
3. Django implements a novel loss function that addresses the problems identified in 2.
4. The paper runs an evaluation against existing techniques and adaptive attacks. 

I like the contribution of this paper proposing a backdoor detection method for object detection models. I don't think this is a groundbreaking contribution, but it is also not a trivial contribution. I believe Django could be a good baseline for backdoor detection in object detection.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. I expect the related work could be a bit more comprehensive in backdoor attacks and detection "in object detection."
2. The evaluation mostly compares the effectiveness against trigger reverse-engineering techniques; comprehensiveness would be nice.

Related work

> Since the paper addresses backdooring in object detection, the related work should discuss the backdoor attacks and defenses in object detection. As backdooring has been studied for a while, there are many backdoor attack papers and proposed defenses. It would also be nice for readers to identify the novelty of this work much more clearly.

Evaluation

> I believe that there are multiple approaches to backdoor defenses (for example, I can think of a simple way like STRIP). Hence, I'd like to see a comprehensive categorization of backdoor defenses (perhaps in the related work section) and a comparison of Django against those missing in the current evaluation. The key idea of this paper is to propose Django, an adaptation of the trigger reverse-engineering technique used for detecting backdoored models in classification to object detection models. The paper shows that existing trigger reverse-engineering techniques are ineffective in object detection and finds that loss misalignment is the primary reason for the less effectiveness. 2. The paper presents why existing detection (trigger reverse-engineering) methods are ineffective.
3. Django implements a novel loss function that addresses the problems identified in 2.

reviews.text:
Here is the rewritten peer review:

## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces Django, an innovative method that adapts trigger reverse-engineering from classification tasks to detect backdoored object detection models. The authors compellingly demonstrate that existing trigger reverse-engineering techniques are largely ineffective in the object detection domain, primarily attributing this deficiency to a misalignment in loss functions. To address this, Django proposes a novel loss function, building upon the well-established Focal loss, specifically designed to identify compromised object detection systems. Through empirical evaluation, Django proves more effective than baseline methods in backdoor detection while also exhibiting lower computational overhead. The study further validates Django's robustness against several adaptive attack scenarios.

**Strengths:**
The strengths of this work are notable:
1.  It presents an original approach for detecting backdoors specifically within object detection models.
2.  The authors effectively diagnose and articulate the limitations of current trigger reverse-engineering methods when applied to object detection, pinpointing loss misalignment as a key factor.
3.  Django's proposed solution, a novel loss function, directly addresses the aforementioned challenges.
4.  The empirical evaluation thoroughly assesses Django's performance against both existing detection techniques and various adaptive attacks.
Overall, I find the core contribution of developing a backdoor detection method for object detection to be valuable. While not revolutionary, it is far from insignificant and has the potential to serve as a strong foundational benchmark for future research in this domain.

**Weaknesses:**
My primary concerns revolve around two areas:
1.  **Related Work Section:** The discussion on prior literature could benefit from a more exhaustive treatment of backdoor attacks and defense mechanisms specifically tailored for object detection. Given the established research in backdooring, a broader overview of existing attacks and defenses relevant to this domain would not only enrich the context but also help readers more clearly discern the unique contributions and novelty of Django.
2.  **Evaluation Scope:** The current experimental comparison predominantly focuses on existing trigger reverse-engineering methods. To provide a more holistic understanding of Django's capabilities, it would be beneficial to broaden the evaluation to include other distinct approaches to backdoor defense (e.g., methods like STRIP, as an example of a different paradigm). This expansion, potentially preceded by a categorization of defense strategies within the related work section, would help to position Django within the broader landscape of backdoor mitigation techniques. The objective here is not necessarily to demonstrate Django's superiority over all possible defenses, but rather to illustrate its relative effectiveness and limitations when benchmarked against a more diverse set of methodologies.

**Questions:**
I have no specific questions for the authors.

**Limitations:**
The paper adequately addresses its limitations within lines 333-342.

**Rating:**
**6: Weak Accept**
This paper presents a technically sound contribution with the potential for moderate-to-high impact. My concerns regarding the comprehensiveness of the related work and evaluation are addressable and do not detract significantly from the core findings or overall quality. The work appears reproducible, and ethical considerations are not a major issue.

## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces an innovative approach for detecting triggers in object detection tasks. The core of the proposed method lies in its novel Gaussian Focus Loss, which is specifically designed to rectify the loss misalignment observed during the trigger inversion process. The experimental evaluations presented in the manuscript thoroughly demonstrate the method's efficacy across various operational environments, including its robustness against adaptive adversarial attacks.

**Strengths:**
1.  A significant contribution of this work is the identification and comprehensive discussion of a generalized phenomenon in object detection model trigger inversion: the observed divergence between Cross-Entropy Loss (CE Loss) and Attack Success Rate (ASR). This insight is particularly valuable and is poised to significantly inform future research efforts in both defending against and understanding backdoor attacks within the object detection paradigm.
2.  The proposed methodology effectively capitalizes on the aforementioned discovery, successfully overcoming the practical challenges associated with its implementation. This demonstrates a strong technical foundation and sound reasoning behind the approach.
3.  The experimental validation is extensive and robust, unequivocally showcasing the high effectiveness of the proposed method. Moreover, its computational overhead is found to be quite acceptable, making it practical for real-world application.
4.  In essence, the presented technique is characterized by its simplicity while simultaneously achieving high effectiveness, and the phenomenon it uncovers offers valuable new perspectives for the broader field.

**Weaknesses:**
A notable omission in the manuscript is the lack of any discussion regarding the inherent limitations of the proposed methodology.

**Questions:**
In line with the identified weakness, the authors are encouraged to explicitly address the limitations of their method within the paper.

**Limitations (from reviewer's perspective):**
While the paper does not explicitly detail its limitations, I believe a critical area for discussion would be the method's potential performance variations when confronted with diverse trigger types, particularly dynamic triggers.

**Rating:**
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the author proposes a new method for detecting backdoors in object detection models. The author finds that directly applying backdoor detection methods for classification models to object detection models results in a loss misalignment problem. Therefore, the author suggests assigning different weights to different bounding boxes during the trigger inversion optimization process. As such, they propose a new optimization loss function in the paper, Gaussian Focus Loss, to better recover triggers. Additionally, to reduce computational overhead, the author also proposes a pre-processing method to decrease the number of label pairs that need to be scanned.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper summarizes and analyzes the loss misalignment phenomenon that occurs during trigger inversion in object detection models and proposes a new optimization function based on this phenomenon. Compared to directly applying existing trigger inversion methods for classification models to object detection models, the method proposed in this paper can achieve better results at a lower cost.
2. The pre-processing method proposed in this paper, based on backdoor leakage, reduces the cost of scanning the model. This method can also be applied to other backdoor detection scenarios.
3. The experimental results in this paper support the effectiveness of the proposed method.
4. The paper is well-organized and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Some hyperparameters mentioned in the paper have not been carefully studied for their impact on the method's effectiveness, such as the two parameters in Eq.4 and the h in label pair pre-processing.
2. All models tested in the paper are selected from TrojanAI, but the paper does not detail the poisoned conditions of the models, such as the poisoning rate, trigger size, and trigger type.
3. There are no recovered trigger samples given in the paper.
4. Code is missing.
reviews.questions:
1. Is this method effective for composite types of triggers? (e.g., "Composite Backdoor Attack for Deep Neural Network by Mixing Existing Benign Features")
2. Is the method proposed in this paper effective for other types of backdoors? (e.g. Backdoors in the physical world "Dangerous Cloaking: Natural Trigger based Backdoor Attacks on Object Detectors in the Physical World")
3. The threat model requires some clean samples; are these samples selected from training samples? If not, would this impact the inversion effectiveness?
4. The paper categorizes backdoor attacks on object detection into two types: misclassification and evasion. Does the misclassification attack include an object-appearing attack, where a bounding box that does not exist in the ground truth appears? ( "BadDet: Backdoor Attacks on Object Detection" and "Clean-image Backdoor: Attacking Multi-label Models with Poisoned Labels Only")
reviews.limitations: The author did not mention limitations in the article. Although this method can detect backdoors in object detection models, it is limited to polygon triggers, and other types of triggers have not been explored.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the author proposes a new method for detecting backdoors in object detection models. The author finds that directly applying backdoor detection methods for classification models to object detection models results in a loss misalignment problem. Therefore, the author suggests assigning different weights to different bounding boxes during the trigger inversion optimization process. As such, they propose a new optimization loss function in the paper, Gaussian Focus Loss, to better recover triggers. Additionally, to reduce computational overhead, the author also proposes a pre-processing method to decrease the number of label pairs that need to be scanned.

Strengths:
1. This paper summarizes and analyzes the loss misalignment phenomenon that occurs during trigger inversion in object detection models and proposes a new optimization function based on this phenomenon. Compared to directly applying existing trigger inversion methods for classification models to object detection models, the method proposed in this paper can achieve better results at a lower cost.
2. The pre-processing method proposed in this paper, based on backdoor leakage, reduces the cost of scanning the model. This method can also be applied to other backdoor detection scenarios.
3. The experimental results in this paper support the effectiveness of the proposed method.
4. The paper is well-organized and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
1. Some hyperparameters mentioned in the paper have not been carefully studied for their impact on the method's effectiveness, such as the two parameters in Eq.4 and the h in label pair pre-processing.
2. All models tested in the paper are selected from TrojanAI, but the paper does not detail the poisoned conditions of the models, such as the poisoning rate, trigger size, and trigger type.
3. There are no recovered trigger samples given in the paper.
4. Code is missing.

Questions:
1. Is this method effective for composite types of triggers? (e.g., "Composite Backdoor Attack for Deep Neural Network by Mixing Existing Benign Features")
2. Is the method proposed in this paper effective for other types of backdoors? (e.g. Backdoors in the physical world "Dangerous Cloaking: Natural Trigger based Backdoor Attacks on Object Detectors in the Physical World")
3. The threat model requires some clean samples; are these samples selected from training samples? If not, would this impact the inversion effectiveness?
4. The paper categorizes backdoor attacks on object detection into two types: misclassification and evasion. Does the misclassification attack include an object-appearing attack, where a bounding box that does not exist in the ground truth appears? ( "BadDet: Backdoor Attacks on Object Detection" and "Clean-image Backdoor: Attacking Multi-label Models with Poisoned Labels Only")

Limitations:
The author did not mention limitations in the article. Although this method can detect backdoors in object detection models, it is limited to polygon triggers, and other types of triggers have not been explored.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
This paper presents a novel methodology for identifying backdoor vulnerabilities within object detection models. The authors pinpoint a "loss misalignment" issue that arises when adapting existing backdoor detection techniques, traditionally designed for classification tasks, directly to the object detection domain. To address this, they propose a strategy involving the differential weighting of bounding boxes during the trigger inversion optimization process. This approach culminates in the introduction of a new optimization function, the Gaussian Focus Loss, aimed at improving trigger recovery. Additionally, to enhance computational efficiency, a pre-processing technique is introduced to reduce the number of label pairs that require scanning.

**Strengths:**

1.  The manuscript effectively articulates and analyzes the phenomenon of loss misalignment encountered during trigger inversion in object detection. Building upon this analysis, it proposes an innovative optimization function that not only achieves superior performance but also operates at a lower computational cost compared to a direct application of classification-oriented trigger inversion methods.
2.  The proposed pre-processing method, which leverages insights from backdoor leakage, significantly reduces the computational expense associated with scanning the model. A notable advantage of this technique is its potential for broader applicability across various backdoor detection scenarios.
3.  The empirical results provided in the paper consistently support the effectiveness and validity of the proposed methodology.
4.  The paper is well-structured, logically organized, and easy to comprehend, contributing to a smooth reading experience.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  A more comprehensive investigation into the sensitivity of the method to certain hyperparameters is warranted. Specifically, the impact of the two parameters within Equation 4 and the 'h' parameter used in the label pair pre-processing on the overall effectiveness of the approach has not been thoroughly explored.
2.  While the experiments utilize models sourced from TrojanAI, the paper lacks crucial details regarding the specific poisoning conditions applied to these models. Information such as the poisoning rate, the dimensions of the trigger, and the precise trigger type used is notably absent.
3.  The submission would benefit from including visual examples of the recovered triggers to provide a clearer illustration of the method's output and capabilities.
4.  The absence of accompanying source code hinders the reproducibility of the research and limits opportunities for further exploration by the community.

**Questions:**

1.  Could the authors elaborate on the method's applicability and effectiveness when confronted with composite trigger types, such as those described in works like "Composite Backdoor Attack for Deep Neural Network by Mixing Existing Benign Features"?
2.  How well does the proposed technique generalize to other categories of backdoors, particularly those encountered in physical-world scenarios, as exemplified by "Dangerous Cloaking: Natural Trigger based Backdoor Attacks on Object Detectors in the Physical World"?
3.  The threat model assumes the availability of certain clean samples. It would be beneficial to clarify whether these samples are drawn from the training dataset. If they are not, an explanation of how their origin might affect the inversion effectiveness is needed.
4.  The paper categorizes backdoor attacks on object detection into "misclassification" and "evasion." Does the "misclassification" category encompass "object-appearing" attacks, where spurious bounding boxes are generated without corresponding ground truth, as discussed in papers like "BadDet: Backdoor Attacks on Object Detection" and "Clean-image Backdoor: Attacking Multi-label Models with Poisoned Labels Only"?

**Limitations:**

While the authors did not explicitly state limitations within the manuscript, it is important to note that the current methodology, despite its efficacy in detecting backdoors in object detection models, appears to be primarily constrained to polygon-shaped triggers, leaving other trigger morphologies unexamined.

**Rating:**

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
The paper proposes the first object detection backdoor detection framework Django (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It leverages a dynamic Gaussian weighting scheme that prioritizes more vulnerable victim boxes and assigns appropriate coefficients to calibrate the optimization objective during trigger inversion.

The experimental results show the superiority of Django over some state-of-the-art baselines.
reviews.strengths:
The authors found that the poison effect can vary significantly across bounding boxes in object detection models due to its dense prediction nature, leading to an undesired optimization objective misalignment issue for existing trigger reverse-engineering methods.

The authors propose a trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection: DJANGO (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It features a Gaussian Focus Loss to calibrate the misaligned loss during inversion by dynamically assigning weights to individual boxes based on their vulnerability. 

The authors claim that equipped with a label proposal pre-processor, DJANGO is able to quickly identify malicious victim-target labels and effectively invert the injected trigger lies in the backdoored model.

The experimental results show Django outperforms some state-of-the-art baselines on four metrics: Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC, and Average Scanning Overheads for each model.
reviews.weaknesses:
Can the authors explain why the authors claim that Django is the first trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection while there have been also many popular and effective trigger inversion backdoor scanning architectures/methods such as [51, 49, 15, 31]? Do you mean that the proposed framework is the first one for object detection? How about [3] mentioned in the paper?

Inspired by the Focal loss [25], the authors propose Gaussian Focus Loss. However, It is unclear (i) how the proposed Gaussian Focus Loss work to solve object detection backdoor detection, and (ii) how the parts in this Gaussian Focus Loss interact to dynamically capture a set of vulnerable boxes that have not been flipped yet, and assign a large coefficient to encourage the transition.

The setting of the training process of the proposed framework and baselines are not mentioned clearly.
reviews.questions:
Please see the questions in the weaknesses section.

Some further questions for improvements:

What is the proposed trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework? Can the authors show a visualization of a flow or a pipeline or an algorithm of this framework including the main components?

Can the authors explain the setting of the training process (e.g., the models configuration and the values of any hyper-parameters used) of the proposed method and baselines?
reviews.limitations: The limitations of the work are not mentioned in the paper.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes the first object detection backdoor detection framework Django (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It leverages a dynamic Gaussian weighting scheme that prioritizes more vulnerable victim boxes and assigns appropriate coefficients to calibrate the optimization objective during trigger inversion.

The experimental results show the superiority of Django over some state-of-the-art baselines.

Strengths:
The authors found that the poison effect can vary significantly across bounding boxes in object detection models due to its dense prediction nature, leading to an undesired optimization objective misalignment issue for existing trigger reverse-engineering methods.

The authors propose a trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection: DJANGO (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It features a Gaussian Focus Loss to calibrate the misaligned loss during inversion by dynamically assigning weights to individual boxes based on their vulnerability. 

The authors claim that equipped with a label proposal pre-processor, DJANGO is able to quickly identify malicious victim-target labels and effectively invert the injected trigger lies in the backdoored model.

The experimental results show Django outperforms some state-of-the-art baselines on four metrics: Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC, and Average Scanning Overheads for each model.

Weaknesses:
Can the authors explain why the authors claim that Django is the first trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection while there have been also many popular and effective trigger inversion backdoor scanning architectures/methods such as [51, 49, 15, 31]? Do you mean that the proposed framework is the first one for object detection? How about [3] mentioned in the paper?

Inspired by the Focal loss [25], the authors propose Gaussian Focus Loss. However, It is unclear (i) how the proposed Gaussian Focus Loss work to solve object detection backdoor detection, and (ii) how the parts in this Gaussian Focus Loss interact to dynamically capture a set of vulnerable boxes that have not been flipped yet, and assign a large coefficient to encourage the transition.

The setting of the training process of the proposed framework and baselines are not mentioned clearly.

Questions:
Please see the questions in the weaknesses section.

Some further questions for improvements:

What is the proposed trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework? Can the authors show a visualization of a flow or a pipeline or an algorithm of this framework including the main components?

Can the authors explain the setting of the training process (e.g., the models configuration and the values of any hyper-parameters used) of the proposed method and baselines?

Limitations:
The limitations of the work are not mentioned in the paper.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Here is the rewritten peer review, maintaining all original critique points, the decision, and the overall tone.

---

**Summary:**
This paper introduces Django (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration), positing it as the inaugural framework for backdoor detection specifically tailored for object detection models using trigger inversion. The approach leverages a dynamic Gaussian weighting mechanism designed to prioritize bounding boxes that are more susceptible to poisoning, thereby appropriately adjusting optimization targets during the trigger inversion process. The empirical evaluation presented in the paper indicates that Django demonstrates superior performance when compared against several established state-of-the-art baselines.

**Strengths:**
The authors have insightfully identified a critical issue: the impact of poisoning can vary significantly across different bounding boxes within object detection models, a consequence of their dense prediction nature. This variability, they argue, leads to a misalignment in the optimization objectives for existing trigger reverse-engineering techniques. To address this, the paper proposes DJANGO, a novel trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework. A core component of DJANGO is its Gaussian Focus Loss, which is designed to rectify the aforementioned misaligned loss during inversion. This is achieved by dynamically assigning weights to individual bounding boxes based on their assessed vulnerability. The authors further assert that, when augmented with a label proposal pre-processor, DJANGO can swiftly pinpoint malicious victim-target labels and effectively reverse-engineer the injected trigger within a compromised model. The experimental results robustly support these claims, showcasing DJANGO's outperformance of several contemporary baselines across four key metrics: Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC, and Average Scanning Overheads per model.

**Weaknesses and Areas for Clarification:**
Several points require further elucidation from the authors:

1.  **Novelty Claim:** The paper states that Django is the first trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection. This claim needs careful justification, especially considering the existence of other prominent and effective trigger inversion backdoor scanning architectures and methodologies, such as those referenced in [51, 49, 15, 31]. The authors should clarify if their claim specifically refers to being the *first for object detection*, and how their work differentiates from or relates to prior work, including reference [3] which is already mentioned in the paper itself.
2.  **Gaussian Focus Loss Mechanism:** While inspired by Focal Loss [25], the operational details of the proposed Gaussian Focus Loss remain somewhat opaque. It is unclear:
    *   (i) How this specific loss function contributes to solving the problem of object detection backdoor detection.
    *   (ii) The precise interaction of its various components to dynamically identify susceptible boxes that have not yet been flipped, and how it subsequently assigns higher coefficients to encourage their transition. A more detailed explanation of its internal workings would greatly benefit clarity.
3.  **Experimental Setup Details:** The paper lacks sufficient detail regarding the training configurations for both the proposed framework and the baseline methods. Specifics such as model architectures, key hyper-parameter values, and other relevant training settings are not clearly articulated, which hinders reproducibility and full understanding of the experimental context.

**Questions for Improvement:**

*   Could the authors provide a visual representation, such as a flowchart, pipeline diagram, or an algorithm, illustrating the overall structure and main components of the proposed trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework?
*   Please elaborate on the complete training process settings, including model configurations and the specific values of all relevant hyper-parameters used for both the proposed method and the baselines in the experiments.

**Limitations:**
The paper does not include a dedicated section discussing the limitations of the proposed work. This omission should be addressed to provide a balanced perspective on the research.

**Rating:**
**5: Borderline accept.** This is a technically sound paper with compelling strengths, particularly in identifying a key issue in object detection backdoor detection and proposing a novel solution. The reasons for acceptance generally outweigh the present concerns, which mainly revolve around clarity, justification of novelty, and completeness of experimental details. Addressing these points would significantly strengthen the manuscript.

Summary:
The paper proposes the first object detection backdoor detection framework Django (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It leverages a dynamic Gaussian weighting scheme that prioritizes more vulnerable victim boxes and assigns appropriate coefficients to calibrate the optimization objective during trigger inversion.

The experimental results show the superiority of Django over some state-of-the-art baselines.

Strengths:
The authors found that the poison effect can vary significantly across bounding boxes in object detection models due to its dense prediction nature, leading to an undesired optimization objective misalignment issue for existing trigger reverse-engineering methods.

The authors propose a trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection: DJANGO (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It features a Gaussian Focus Loss to calibrate the misaligned loss during inversion by dynamically assigning weights to individual boxes based on their vulnerability. 

The authors claim that equipped with a label proposal pre-processor, DJANGO is able to quickly identify malicious victim-target labels and effectively invert the injected trigger lies in the backdoored model.

The experimental results show Django outperforms some state-of-the-art baselines on four metrics: Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC, and Average Scanning Overheads for each model.

Weaknesses:
Can the authors explain why the authors claim that Django is the first trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection while there have been also many popular and effective trigger inversion backdoor scanning architectures/methods such as [51, 49, 15, 31]? Do you mean that the proposed framework is the first one for object detection? How about [3] mentioned in the paper?

Inspired by the Focal loss [25], the authors propose Gaussian Focus Loss. However, It is unclear (i) how the proposed Gaussian Focus Loss work to solve object detection backdoor detection, and (ii) how the parts in this Gaussian Focus Loss interact to dynamically capture a set of vulnerable boxes that have not been flipped yet, and assign a large coefficient to encourage the transition.

The setting of the training process of the proposed framework and baselines are not mentioned clearly.

Questions:
Please see the questions in the weaknesses section.

Some further questions for improvements:

What is the proposed trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework? Can the authors show a visualization of a flow or a pipeline or an algorithm of this framework including the main components?

Can the authors explain the setting of the training process (e.g., the models configuration and the values of any hyper-parameters used) of the proposed method and baselines?

Limitations:
The limitations of the work are not mentioned in the paper.
